request.question: Susan May Pratt played the part of Mandella in a movie that came out in which year?

title: 10 Things I Hate About You
Susan May Pratt
Lenora Mandella
Jerome Napoleon Bonaparte II
Black-eyed Susan
Open Water 2: Adrift
Susan May Williams
Imogen Boorman
Andy Pratt (baseball)
Kyla Pratt
sentences: 10 Things I Hate About You is a 1999 American romantic comedy film directed by Gil Junger and starring Julia Stiles, Heath Ledger, Joseph Gordon-Levitt, and Larisa Oleynik.

The screenplay, written by Karen McCullah Lutz and Kirsten Smith, is a modernization of William Shakespeare's late-16th century comedy "The Taming of the Shrew", retold in a late-1990s American high school setting.

In the story, new student Cameron (Gordon-Levitt) is smitten with Bianca (Oleynik) and, in order to get around her father's strict rules on dating, attempts to get bad boy Patrick (Ledger) to date Bianca's ill-tempered sister, Kat (Stiles).
The film is titled after a poem written by Kat about her bittersweet romance with Patrick.
Much of the filming took place in the Seattle metropolitan area, with many scenes shot at Stadium High School in Tacoma.
Susan May Pratt (born February 8, 1974) is an American actress.
She played Mandella in "10 Things I Hate About You", Alicia in "Drive Me Crazy", and Maureen Cummings in "Center Stage".
Lenora Mandella (4 May 1931 - 12 August 2005) played shortstop and pitcher in the All-American Girls Professional Baseball League between 1949 and 1951.
She both batted and threw right-handed.
Her nickname was Smokey.
She measured in at 5 feet 7 inches and weighed 145 pounds.
Jerome-Napoleon Bonaparte II (1830-1893) was an American soldier who served in the French Army.
He was the son of Jérôme Napoléon Bonaparte and Susan May Williams.
Black-eyed Susan may refer to one of several things:

Open Water 2: Adrift (called Adrift in the UK, Australia, and various other countries, and called Open Water 2 in Germany) is a 2006 psychological horror film filmed entirely in Malta, starring Eric Dane, Susan May Pratt, Richard Speight, Jr., Niklaus Lange, Ali Hillis, and Cameron Richardson.
Promotional posters claim the film is based on actual events.

Susan May Williams Bonaparte (April 2, 1812 – September 15, 1881) was the daughter of Benjamin Williams, a prominent Baltimore merchant (originally from Roxbury, Massachusetts), and his wife, Sarah Copeland, widow of Nathaniel Morton.

In response to the opening of the Erie Canal, which was in direct competition with the port of Baltimore, her father became one of the founders of the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad, the first railroad company in the United States, chartered on April 24, 1827.
Imogen May Pratt Boorman (born 13 May 1971) is an English film actress and television actress.
She is known for portraying Tiffany in the horror film "", Lorina in "Dreamchild", Clothhide in "May to December" and Hannah Preston in "Westbeach".
Andrew Elias Pratt (born August 27, 1979) is a former Major League Baseball pitcher who played for the Atlanta Braves and Chicago Cubs.
Pratt was selected by the Texas Rangers in the 9th round of the 1998 Major League Baseball Draft and had a career ERA of 15.00 in 5 career appearances with the Braves and Cubs.
He also played in the Texas Rangers organization from - and Milwaukee Brewers organization from -.
After his release from the Brewers organization in 2006, Pratt played the rest of the season for the Somerset Patriots of the independent Atlantic League.
He has not played professionally since.
Kyla Alissa Pratt is an American actress.

In television, she is known for her roles in the popular U.S. children's television series "Barney & Friends" and the Disney Channel series "The Proud Family" (which lasted for 3 seasons) and "One on One" (which lasted for 5 seasons).

After playing the daughter of Eddie Murphy's character in the films "Dr. Dolittle" and "Dr. Dolittle 2", Pratt became the main character in the remake series of the franchise such as "Dr. Dolittle 3", "", and "".
Pratt has also been in the films "Fat Albert", "Hotel for Dogs", and "The Proud Family Movie".
She has also played in the series "Let's Stay Together".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate named in the question has no grounded mention in any provided passage, explicitly mark it as NOT present under evidence.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge / bridge_entity_or_missing_fact: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation. Use a slight conditional labeling here:
      * If the question names an entity that has no grounded mention in the provided passages and the unresolved gap is identity/disambiguation (you cannot map the question token to a single canonical entity needed to ground later facts), label the gap as `bridge_entity_or_missing_fact` and specify that the canonical/biography page for that named entity is required to resolve identity.
      * Otherwise (the missing premise is a concrete attribute/property such as a date, developer, layout engine, filmography item, opening-order token, etc.), use `missing_facts_or_bridge` and describe the exact attribute(s) required (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "developer of Product X"). Do not request the canonical biography page by default in this case; prefer an attribute-targeted retrieval first.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Order targets by priority and keep them short. Apply this retrieval-priority conditional:
      * If the missing gap is identity/disambiguation (see bridge_entity_or_missing_fact above), put the candidate's canonical/biography page title as retrieval_direction #1 (exact title if known) and include 1–2 biography-style fallback queries (e.g., "<Name> biography", "<Name> official page") as subsequent targets.
      * If the missing gap is a specific attribute/property, put 1–2 concise attribute-focused queries first (e.g., "<Item> developer", "<Item> layout engine", "<Work B> publication year"). Include the canonical/biography page as a secondary target only if it might reasonably contain the attribute and identity resolution could still be needed.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- Narrowed applicability for birth-date disambiguation: If summary_1 lacks a confirmed bridge AND the question explicitly requires a birth-date comparison (e.g., asks who was born earlier or requests birth years) AND the visible inputs (question or summary_1) show ambiguous name candidates AND a suitable disambiguator token (occupation, band, institution, or a parenthetical label) is present or clearly implied in the visible inputs, then emit one compact query that combines: the ambiguous name anchor, the disambiguator copied verbatim from the visible inputs, and a concise birth-term ("born" or "birth year"). In all other cases (including birth-name mappings, page-title targets, non-temporal bridges, or when no usable disambiguator is available), do not append birth/date relation words or invent disambiguators; instead prefer the minimal visible-anchor form (page-title, name-pair, or person+institution) appropriate to the missing bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If the conditional birth-date disambiguation rule above applies, build the query by concatenating: the ambiguous person name, the disambiguator token copied verbatim from the visible inputs (occupation, band, institution, parenthetical), and a single concise birth-term ("born" or "birth year"). Use only the visible disambiguator wording; do not invent or normalize parentheticals.
- Relation-token selection rule: choose a single concise relation token that matches the question's relation (e.g., "born" or "birth year" only for temporal birth queries). Do not append relation tokens when the canonical retrieval strategy is to use a page-title, exact name-pair, or person+institution anchor.
- Do not include the final answer or explanatory text. If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Append the primary canonical label/token from `context` and at most one short passage-grounded supporting sentence only when a passage contains an explicit, directly stated predicate that links the same entity token from `context` (or a clearly corroborated alias/role/date) to the partner or attribute the question cites. Examples of acceptable explicit predicates include clear statements like 'X with Y', 'X is a member of Y', 'X was born DATE', 'X is a recipient of AWARD', 'X is also called Z', or 'X began tenure in YEAR'. The trigger requires that the passage (a) names the same entity token as in `context` or an alias/role/date that is corroborated by other attributes in `context`, and (b) explicitly asserts the relation or attribute needed by the question. Do not append when the passage only contains the attribute string without an explicit predicate, a nearby mention, an indirect implication, or when the link refers to a different named entity.

- Transitive alias/composition: Do not assert transitive equivalence unless every hop in the chain is explicitly stated as an equivalence in the supplied passages and each hop clearly refers to the same entity. If only a mediated or inferred chain exists (i.e., intermediate links are not explicit equivalences and identity-aligned), suppress the appended support and preserve uncertainty.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. Permit an exception for identification/single-token/binary cases only when the question's semantics truly demand the relation/attribute (for example, determining an era boundary or tenure date) and a passage explicitly provides the necessary predicate linking the context entity to that temporal relation or attribute.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` by matching names, corroborating aliases, roles, dates, or other attributes. If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead. Avoid naive substring or proximity matches as sufficient evidence.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Preserve the exact canonical label/token selected from `context`. Use a compact paraphrase grounded in passage language, avoid inventing facts or long verbatim quotations, and avoid misattributing attributes from different entities.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

When multiple co-referent surface strings for the same entity appear in summary_2, you may prefer a shorter canonical label only if that exact shorter string appears verbatim in summary_2 either as an independent label or as an explicit parenthetical alternate, AND emitting that shorter string alone would not drop administrative or category qualifiers, required punctuation/abbreviations, or other disambiguating tokens that the question requires; otherwise emit the minimal multi-token or formatted string exactly as supported by summary_2. Preserve multi-token formatted place names, parenthetical acronyms or alternates when they are present or when the question's intent relies on them.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

sentences: 10 Things I Hate About You is a 1999 American romantic comedy film directed by Gil Junger and starring Julia Stiles, Heath Ledger, Joseph Gordon-Levitt, and Larisa Oleynik.

The screenplay, written by Karen McCullah Lutz and Kirsten Smith, is a modernization of William Shakespeare's late-16th century comedy "The Taming of the Shrew", retold in a late-1990s American high school setting.

In the story, new student Cameron (Gordon-Levitt) is smitten with Bianca (Oleynik) and, in order to get around her father's strict rules on dating, attempts to get bad boy Patrick (Ledger) to date Bianca's ill-tempered sister, Kat (Stiles).
The film is titled after a poem written by Kat about her bittersweet romance with Patrick.
Much of the filming took place in the Seattle metropolitan area, with many scenes shot at Stadium High School in Tacoma.
Susan May Pratt (born February 8, 1974) is an American actress.
She played Mandella in "10 Things I Hate About You", Alicia in "Drive Me Crazy", and Maureen Cummings in "Center Stage".
Lenora Mandella (4 May 1931 - 12 August 2005) played shortstop and pitcher in the All-American Girls Professional Baseball League between 1949 and 1951.
She both batted and threw right-handed.
Her nickname was Smokey.
She measured in at 5 feet 7 inches and weighed 145 pounds.
Jerome-Napoleon Bonaparte II (1830-1893) was an American soldier who served in the French Army.
He was the son of Jérôme Napoléon Bonaparte and Susan May Williams.
Black-eyed Susan may refer to one of several things:

Open Water 2: Adrift (called Adrift in the UK, Australia, and various other countries, and called Open Water 2 in Germany) is a 2006 psychological horror film filmed entirely in Malta, starring Eric Dane, Susan May Pratt, Richard Speight, Jr., Niklaus Lange, Ali Hillis, and Cameron Richardson.
Promotional posters claim the film is based on actual events.

Susan May Williams Bonaparte (April 2, 1812 – September 15, 1881) was the daughter of Benjamin Williams, a prominent Baltimore merchant (originally from Roxbury, Massachusetts), and his wife, Sarah Copeland, widow of Nathaniel Morton.

In response to the opening of the Erie Canal, which was in direct competition with the port of Baltimore, her father became one of the founders of the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad, the first railroad company in the United States, chartered on April 24, 1827.
Imogen May Pratt Boorman (born 13 May 1971) is an English film actress and television actress.
She is known for portraying Tiffany in the horror film "", Lorina in "Dreamchild", Clothhide in "May to December" and Hannah Preston in "Westbeach".
Andrew Elias Pratt (born August 27, 1979) is a former Major League Baseball pitcher who played for the Atlanta Braves and Chicago Cubs.
Pratt was selected by the Texas Rangers in the 9th round of the 1998 Major League Baseball Draft and had a career ERA of 15.00 in 5 career appearances with the Braves and Cubs.
He also played in the Texas Rangers organization from - and Milwaukee Brewers organization from -.
After his release from the Brewers organization in 2006, Pratt played the rest of the season for the Somerset Patriots of the independent Atlantic League.
He has not played professionally since.
Kyla Alissa Pratt is an American actress.

In television, she is known for her roles in the popular U.S. children's television series "Barney & Friends" and the Disney Channel series "The Proud Family" (which lasted for 3 seasons) and "One on One" (which lasted for 5 seasons).

After playing the daughter of Eddie Murphy's character in the films "Dr. Dolittle" and "Dr. Dolittle 2", Pratt became the main character in the remake series of the franchise such as "Dr. Dolittle 3", "", and "".
Pratt has also been in the films "Fat Albert", "Hotel for Dogs", and "The Proud Family Movie".
She has also played in the series "Let's Stay Together".

response.hop1_query: Susan May Pratt played the part of Mandella in a movie that came out in which year?
hop1_docs: Susan May Pratt | Susan May Pratt (born February 8, 1974) is an American actress.  She played Mandella in "10 Things I Hate About You", Alicia in "Drive Me Crazy", and Maureen Cummings in "Center Stage".

Seattle Sun (2002–05) | The Seattle Sun was a free, monthly neighborhood newspaper in Seattle, Washington.  Founded in 1997 by Clayton and Susan Brehme Park as the Jet City Maven, its first issue came out in March of that year.  The next issue came out in May, and beginning in July, the paper went monthly.  It originally covered only that part of Seattle north of N.E. 65th Street and east of Aurora Avenue N., but in September 1998 it was expanded to include all of the city north of the Lake Washington Ship Canal.

Lenora Mandella | Lenora Mandella (4 May 1931 - 12 August 2005) played shortstop and pitcher in the All-American Girls Professional Baseball League between 1949 and 1951.  She both batted and threw right-handed.  Her nickname was Smokey.  She measured in at 5 feet 7 inches and weighed 145 pounds.
Susan Pratt | Susan Pratt (born April 29, 1956) is an American actress.

Kyla Pratt | Kyla Alissa Pratt is an American actress.  In television, she is known for her roles in the popular U.S. children's television series "Barney & Friends" and the Disney Channel series "The Proud Family" (which lasted for 3 seasons) and "One on One" (which lasted for 5 seasons).  After playing the daughter of Eddie Murphy's character in the films "Dr. Dolittle" and "Dr. Dolittle 2", Pratt became the main character in the remake series of the franchise such as "Dr. Dolittle 3", "", and "".  Pratt has also been in the films "Fat Albert", "Hotel for Dogs", and "The Proud Family Movie".  She has also played in the series "Let's Stay Together".

Susan Peters (Nigerian actress) | Susan Peters (born May 30, 1980) is a multiple award-winning Nigerian actress with over 50 credits in Nollywood (Nigerian) movies.  She is a star on Nigerian TV, a successful model, interior designer and beauty salon owner.  Recently, she won the 2011 Afro Hollywood Best Actress (English) Award for her role in "Bursting Out", NAFCA Awards (Nollywood and African Film Critics Awards) North Carolina Nigerian Oscars: Best Actress in Supporting Role 2011 the BON (Best Of Nollywood) Best Supporting Actress Award 2011, and the Actress of the Year 2010 and Most Stylish Actress 2012 Awards from "City People Magazine".  In 2011, she made the December cover of the creatively acclaimed, arts and culture magazine, "Zen".  Said "Zen Magazine" editor, Arinze Nwokolo, "Susan Peters is a remarkable and an incredible talent in the movie industry!  Her dedication to her art is unmistakable..."

The Pedagogical Poem | The Pedagogical Poem (Russian: Педагогическая поэма , "Pedagogicheskaya poema " ), translated into English as "Road to Life", is a novel by Anton Makarenko, written in 1925-1935 and published in three parts in 1933-1935.  Part 1 of the novel came out in 1933 (in the Year XVII Almanac, part 3), Part 2 in 1935 (Year XVIII Almanac, book 5) and part 3 later the same year (Year XVIII Almanac, book 8).  As a separate edition, the book came out 1937, through Khudozhestvennaya Literatura.

response.hop2_query: 10 Things I Hate About You release year
hop2_docs: 10 Things I Hate About You (TV series) | 10 Things I Hate About You is an American television sitcom broadcast on ABC Family beginning in 2009.  Developed by Carter Covington, the show is a half-hour, single camera series based on "Ten Things I Hate About You".  It premiered on Tuesday, July 7, 2009 at 8 pm and brought in 1.60 million viewers, a record for a 30-minute comedy debut on the ABC Family network.

10 Things I Hate About Life | 10 Things I Hate About Life is an cancelled/unfinished American romantic comedy film directed and written by Gil Junger.  The film stars Evan Rachel Wood, Thomas McDonell and Billy Campbell.

7 Things | "7 Things" is a song by American singer-songwriter and actress Miley Cyrus.  The song was co-written by Cyrus, Antonina Armato and Tim James, and produced by John Fields.  It was released on June 17, 2008 by Hollywood Records as the lead single from the first studio album released by Cyrus with no ties to her character Hannah Montana from the Disney Channel comedy series of the same name.  Also registered as "Seven Things I Hate About You", Cyrus developed the song during the Best of Both Worlds Tour while feeling numerous emotions for an ex-boyfriend, mainly anger.  The song's release brought allegations that it was about Nick Jonas of the Jonas Brothers, which Cyrus neither confirmed nor denied.  Musically, "7 Things" bears aspects of both country and pop punk, while the lyrics in the song's refrain list seven traits Cyrus hates about an ex-boyfriend.
Sexy Boy (Air song) | "Sexy Boy" is a single by French band Air and was featured in the film "10 Things I Hate About You" as well as the British television series "Queer as Folk".

Change (Miho Fukuhara song) | "Change" is the major-label debut single by Japanese soul singer Miho Fukuhara, telling of a girl who talks playfully about she loves a man, going as far as saying things like "I hate, hate, hate you!  Just kidding, I love you!" .  The single was released on April 16, 2008 to decent success regarding both sales and airplay, peaking at #14 on Oricon and #4 on Billboard Japan.  "Change" is still currently Fukuhara's best-selling single.

10 Things I Hate About You | 10 Things I Hate About You is a 1999 American romantic comedy film directed by Gil Junger and starring Julia Stiles, Heath Ledger, Joseph Gordon-Levitt, and Larisa Oleynik.  The screenplay, written by Karen McCullah Lutz and Kirsten Smith, is a modernization of William Shakespeare's late-16th century comedy "The Taming of the Shrew", retold in a late-1990s American high school setting.  In the story, new student Cameron (Gordon-Levitt) is smitten with Bianca (Oleynik) and, in order to get around her father's strict rules on dating, attempts to get bad boy Patrick (Ledger) to date Bianca's ill-tempered sister, Kat (Stiles).  The film is titled after a poem written by Kat about her bittersweet romance with Patrick.  Much of the filming took place in the Seattle metropolitan area, with many scenes shot at Stadium High School in Tacoma.

Dana Davis | Dana Davis (born October 4, 1978) is an American actress, known for playing Chastity Church on the ABC Family television series "10 Things I Hate About You" (2009–10) and Carmen Phillips on the TNT series "Franklin & Bash" (2011-2013).
hop2_titles: 10 Things I Hate About You (TV series)
response.summary_2: Susan May Pratt — "10 Things I Hate About You" was released in 1999.
